As part of the international consortium project “Power-Oriented Natural Graphite for Advanced Battery Systems”, this project will demonstrate the potential of graphite process fines for battery use. Figure 1 (see on attached proposal) shows the NGAM production flow for LIB anodes, starting with mining and beneficiation to extract flake graphite. After crushing, grinding, and flotation, purity reaches =97%. Micronization (6–20?µm) and spheroidization improve shape and performance but generate ~50% low-value fines. These are typically discarded. RAINCA and NGCBM aim to revalorize them into high-performance, battery-grade materials, supporting sustainable graphite use.

detail:
This project will enable Phase Sensors to reduce drift in its pressure sensors.  Drift is a critical specification for precision pressure sensors as it directly impacts the accuracy of a measurement over time.  Sensors are typically re-calibrated at schedules determined by sensor drift rates.  A lower drift rate means the sensor is more accurate for a longer period of time and does not need to be re-calibrated as often.

Phase Sensors has demonstrated better drift performance than its main competitor at 25°C and 10,000psi.  Initial testing between 20,000psi and 30,000psi indicate that further work needs to be done in sensor design to reduce drift rates at very high pressure.

detail: In the electric steelmaking route, the Electric Arc Furnace process is the most critical phase in terms of energy consumption, metallic losses, and overall cost. One of the main challenges is the limited knowledge of the characteristics of the charged scrap and its melting behavior inside the furnace, as well as the condition of the slag and molten steel bath. Tallman is developing a permeant in-furnace vision system and sensors for monitoring furnace and slag conditions and scrap melting, allowing the operators greater control over the process, not previously possible.

detail:
The use of automated prognostic imaging biomarkers in clinical practice may enhance (re-) staging and response assessment, as it could inform more personalized treatment selection and patient monitoring, which in turn could lead to improved patient outcomes. The purpose of this project is to develop automated, deep learning-based imaging biomarkers that predict outcomes of breast cancer patients with solid tumors based on various modalities of medical imaging. 
By enabling an artificial neural network to “learn” what visual features in thousands of imaging studies are associated with respective patients’ actual future outcomes, the resulting deep learning algorithm will be able to predict survival for a new patient’s imaging study.

detail: This project aims to develop and validate a novel platform for laser-assisted targeted drug delivery using LMI's proprietary picosecond mid-infrared laser technology. This technology enables precise ablation of tissues and when coupled to a microfluidic drug channel, the optic probe can deliver localized therapeutics with high resolution and minimal invasiveness. LMI holds foundational IP specific to this application covering fiberoptic laser-drug delivery systems in addition to the original core IP portfolio covering the unique laser tissue interaction. The platform will be validated in, phantom tissue, ex vivo and animal models, focusing on applications in high-value organ systems: prostate, brain, lung, and pancreas.

detail:
The GIST is a women-operated sports media brand, with a community of over 1 million fans who have been underserved by traditional sports media.

Our community spans many channels, including multiple newsletters, job board, contests, games, podcast, social media, e-commerce, membership, and live events. Currently, user data is siloed within the different channels, and our pipeline for storing and analyzing data is almost entirely centered around our newsletters.

The goal of this project is to create a unified profile for our users and data team, and rework the data pipeline to aggregate and report on user activity. This will give us a more detailed and holistic view of who our users are via their behaviors and preferences.

detail: Leaky blood vessels in the retina are the main cause of vision loss in diseases like age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy – which collectively affect 380 million individuals worldwide. While there are drugs that can stop retinal leakage, the most reliable test for detecting it involves an injection of dye and necessitates emergency readiness in case of an allergic reaction. Hence, this test is invasive, complex, costly, and infrequently used. To address this challenge, we propose an innovative artificial intelligence (AI) approach that will simulate the invasive test from a non-invasive, injection-free photograph commonly collected in routine eye exams.

detail: The aim of the CIIP - UK Multisector - BT-XC-UTI project is to discover a new medicine to treat urinary tract infections. It is a collaboration between a Canadian company (IntelliSyn) with expertise in chemistry and a British company with expertise in biology (BioTryp). BioTryp has identified a bacterial protein that plays a key role in infection. IntelliSyn will design new therapeutic agents that inhibit this protein, and BioTryp will quantify the extent of inhibition in their biological assays.  This iterative optimization cycle will also include drug elimination and safety optimization such that a safe, oral medicine to cure urinary tract infections can be identified.

detail:
Vaultie is developing a Bifurcated Credential which is a digital verifiable credential that can be verified by any party but a subset of the information can only be verified by specific parties. This allow for instances where an issuer can issue credentials with gated information that could be verified at each specific gate, for example, a health card which may need to verified for its authenticity by a medical clerk, but may need specific information for perhaps a doctor or pharmacist. This type of double use verification has many use cases that can be further explored and can foster faster adoption and interoperability of credentials by enabling combined economic models.
This differs from a Zero Knowledge Proof as a Bifurcated Credential

The Project is a collaboration between QuantaSense and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC) to develop broadband, quantum-limited amplifiers (QLAs) for cryogenic radio astronomy receivers. Using kinetic inductance technology, these superconducting amplifiers will operate in the 4–12 GHz range (with potential extension to 16 GHz), targeting sub-2K noise temperatures and flat, stable gain. The Project addresses Canada’s current reliance on foreign suppliers for ultra-low-noise amplifiers by establishing a sovereign, scalable capability in quantum-limited signal amplification. The Project will begin with joint definition of performance specifications, followed by QuantaSense-led design, simulation, and cleanroom fabrication of prototype devices. Packaged amplifiers will be cryogenically tested and benchmarked by NRC against conventional HEMT-based amplifiers. Feedback will guide iterative design improvements and optimization for realworld applications. Expected outcomes include a validated QLA prototype, a documented nanofabrication process for scalable production, and comparative performance data. These will enable commercialization efforts targeting astronomy, quantum sensi
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The project will develop and industrialize nickel‑rich layered‑oxide cathode active materials that deliver high-energy density with reduced cobalt content, and will validate their performance in industry‑relevant cell formats. Hydro-Québec’s Centre of Excellence for Transportation Electrification and Energy Storage and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC) will collaborate on material design, pilot synthesis, electrode engineering and cell manufacturing to accelerate scale‑up. The NRC’s efforts will focus on controlled particle morphology, compositional tuning and surface engineering via targeted dopants and conformal coatings, while the Centre will optimize slurry recipes and continuous roll‑to‑roll coating, drying and calendering to produce uniform, high‑quality electrodes. Parallel workstreams will develop low‑impact recycling routes and demonstrate the incorporation of recycled nickel‑rich active material into complete cells, supporting a sustainable pathway to commercialization.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

CarbonAi is developing a platform called SPARK on top of its existing digital infrastructure known as FIELD AND PROOF to deliver verifiable, traceable environmental outcomes that support carbon credits, emissions reporting, and transition finance. 

FIELD is a platform that enables structured, field-level data capture and integration with connected devices and PROOF transforms captured data into outcome-ready, cryptographically verifiable records. 

SPARK is purpose-built for clean cooking programs that require statistically robust, audit-ready data to support large-scale initiatives. It integrates sensors and mobile tools with real-time validation workflows to ensure continuous monitoring and outcome generation, even in remote or low-connectivity environments.

Leaky blood vessels in the retina are the main cause of vision loss in diseases like age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy – which collectively affect 380 million individuals worldwide. While there are drugs that can stop retinal leakage, the most reliable test for detecting it involves an injection of dye and necessitates emergency readiness in case of an allergic reaction. Hence, this test is invasive, complex, costly, and infrequently used. To address this challenge, we propose an innovative artificial intelligence (AI) approach that will simulate the invasive test from a non-invasive, injection-free photograph commonly collected in routine eye exams.

Melanin is a pigment polymer found in the skin, hair, and eyes, essential for coloration and protecting tissues from UV damage. Naturally synthesized in the human body and by many other organisms across several varieties of species (e.g. bacteria, fungi, mammals, etc); it can also be chemically synthesized or produced via microbial fermentation. Chemically synthesized melanin is considered an environmentally taxing and financially expensive process.

The Black Soldier Fly (BSF) and its pupae is one such organism from which the naturally occurring melanin can be extracted. Oberland, in collaboration with a third-party contractor, seeks to optimize the melanin extraction and purifications processes, first at laboratory scale and then reaching pilot scale.

CycurID Technologies Ltd. will evaluate its readiness to enter the Canadian defence market with a production-proven, quantum-resistant communication layer featuring one-time-use encrypted tunnels and API obfuscation. The project will assess the competitive landscape, defence procurement pathways, and regulatory requirements including Controlled Goods, security clearances, and cryptographic certifications (FIPS 140-3, Common Criteria). A technical gap analysis will identify adaptations needed to meet military-grade standards. This work directly supports Canada's "Our North, Strong and Free" defence policy priorities in cyber resilience and quantum threat mitigation.

The project develops an understanding of the business opportunity for an existing domestic AI image processing solution in support of defence mobility and infrastructure priorities as outlined in multiple Department of Defence plans and vision documents. Diseased blood vessels in the retina are the main cause of vision loss in diseases like age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy, which collectively affect 380 million individuals worldwide. The primary mechanisms that drive vision loss in these diseases are well-known: (i) leakage from retinal blood vessels, and (ii) reduced blood supply to retinal tissue.
The aim is ultimately to develop a robust method for measuring leakage from retinal blood vessels and reduced retinal blood supply, to allow accurate monitoring of these abnormalities during patient follow-up and in response to treatment.

Baüne will establish and document the requirements and processes needed to deploy AVACS™ an offline‑first autonomous medical platform. The project defines personnel, facility, and product‑level safeguards.

Creation and integration of data capture and analysis software to increase efficiency, quality and productivity in production of dual use iron castings.  This technology will result in more consistent higher output and lower costs. This will improve value proposition to OEM's for both commercial and defence applications allowing for a sovereign source to be more cost competitive against overseas sources.

Manufacturing in Canada, GBatteries’ technology directly align with CAF and NATO priorities, including energy resilience, Arctic operability, and electrification of unmanned and autonomous platforms. Lumera Solar identified an opportunity to develop a deployable solar array for use within resource mining and secondary military operations. Electrical power generation in remote locations can be challenging, with current solutions requiring fuel or easily damaged flexible solar panels. Lumera Solar intends to research military markets, including specific needs, procurement processes, storage capacities, portability requirements, product durability, and distribution stipulations in order to better prepare for product research and development.

This Phase 1 project focuses on building foundational AI-assisted fraud analysis capabilities within the NetNow platform. The scope includes early fraud signal identification, real-time risk scoring at application submission, and surfacing fraud-related indicators within the credit review workflow. These outputs are intended to support human-led decisions. Future enhancements may build on this Phase 1 foundation as follow-on work beyond the currently supported scope.

Waste Parrot, a Canadian company, won Phase 1 of NASA’s Lunar Recycling Challenge, beating 1,200 teams from 86 countries. Its modular autonomous system uses AI material characterization to turn plastics and other non-metabolic waste into reusable resources for long missions. Unlike U.S. winners, international teams received no cash award, leaving Waste Parrot without funds to advance to Phase 2 underway. Support would keep Wasteparrot represented and move space-proven recycling into remote terrestrial use. Phase 2 targets a deployable prototype in 8 months that sorts with AI vision and robotics and 3D-prints tools, insulation, and structural parts. Partners include: EWMC, BioEnergy Solutions Inc. and REAC-Indigenous community pilots.

The Iqaluit Inuit Owned Lands Microgrid Demonstration Project is being developed by Nunavut Nukkiksautit Corporation at the Aqsarniit Hotel and Conference Centre in Iqaluit, Nunavut. The hotel currently relies on utility electricity from Qulliq Energy Corporation and an oil boiler for heat. The project includes 250 kW of solar PV, a 250 kW/535 kWh battery energy storage system, and two 400 kW diesel generators integrated with an air-to-water heat pump - the first of its kind in Nunavut. Together, the system will supply 100% of the hotel’s electrical and thermal load while reducing diesel use.

This project will prepare a defence-focused software system for adoption by military users by identifying the most viable path to defence buyers and operational deployment. The project will validate and prioritize main Command and Control and situational awareness use cases - secure dissemination, data provenance and integrity verification, and edge optimization for constrained links - and define the procurement, integration, and partnership pathways necessary to bring the system to the Canadian and allied defence customers.

This project will assess the defence readiness of a Canadian-developed digital identity documentation system designed to support traceability and accountability in complex operational environments. The work will examine the technical, operational, and regulatory requirements needed to adapt the technology for defence applications. Activities will include analysis of defence operational needs, system architecture considerations, security and compliance requirements, and potential procurement pathways. The project will produce a defence readiness roadmap outlining the steps required to align the technology with defence sector requirements and future evaluation opportunities.

This project is a half-year technology sprint meant to rapidly advance the operational readiness of AlumaPower’s aluminum-fueled generator technology for military and dual-use applications, in support of upcoming trial and demo events. This effort builds on recent military-focused R&D activities and addresses technical and conceptual feedback from Canadian and allied end users. Activities include: accelerated testing to validate system / sub-system performance and generate a consolidated evidence package; maturation of next-generation, high-energy-density aluminum-air galvanic cells; development of optional modular thermal management and heat export concepts; and preparation of prototype units for packaging, shipment, and off-site trials.

Deep Breathe is developing portable, turnkey, novice-friendly devices to assist warfighters with rapid trauma triage. The long-term vision is to equip military personnel with a simple, screenless ultrasound device with onboard algorithms that detect life-threatening consequences of traumatic injury. This all-in-one device, named “ATLAS Probe”, will revolutionize battlefield diagnostic capabilities. Given the long time required to design and manufacture hardware, we are developing ATLAS Tactical – a mobile application that interfaces with handheld ultrasound devices, functioning as a comprehensive trauma assessment platform. Lastly, we are developing an educational mobile app featuring early prototypes of the algorithms in ATLAS Tactical.

This project will assess the feasibility of integrating AI-based diagnostic tools using LoRaWAN communication protocols into existing Wi-Fi mesh networks, optimized for harsh Arctic conditions. The study will focus on developing and validating predictive diagnostic algorithms, hybrid network nodes, and real-time monitoring capabilities to improve network reliability, reduce downtime, and support critical infrastructure in remote regions. Field trials will be conducted in Inuvik, NT using existing infrastructure, with outcomes informing IP strategy and commercialization planning for Canadian and global markets.

The project builds a foundation for continuous learning and operational resilience across industry and defense.

We are revolutionizing knowledge capture for industrial and defense sectors with an AI-driven platform powered by Vision-Language Models. The system transforms raw video from smart glasses, cameras, mobiles, and drones into a searchable library of concise, AI-generated procedural videos. By recording real-world workflows as they're performed, the AI can produce standard operating procedure videos. Features include natural language and image search, precise moment retrieval, and deployed on cloud or self-hosted for security and scale. This is the next frontier of AI, driving continuous learning and operational resilience.

Develop survey-to-survey registration and change detection capabilities integrated with the 3D sonar and BathyGraph software platform to enable repeatable alignment and comparison of underwater sonar surveys to detect changes in seabed terrain and structures with quantified confidence. Aviridi is undertaking an R&D project to develop a large-format hot press platen and tooling system for advanced molded fiber manufacturing. The project focuses on analyzing and managing thermal expansion in a 1.2 m × 1.2 m platen used in pulforming equipment, the largest size currently deployed in the molded fiber industry. The work includes engineering design improvements, development of tooling fixtures, and software enhancements for robotic automation to maintain alignment during high-temperature operation. Kapoose Creek Bio will adapt our existing AI-enabled phenotypic profiling platform for chemical threat assessment. Unlike chemistry-first detection that requires knowing what to look for, our activity-based approach identifies biological mechanism of action regardless of chemical novelty detecting even never-before-seen agents, such as novel derivatives that would evade traditional methods. The platform uses high-content cell imaging combined with Random Forest machine learning, leveraging a training set of 13,000+ bioactive compounds with known activity and mechanism. We will build a reference atlas of ~100 threat-relevant agents to further train mechanism classification. This capability will provide actionable intelligence within 48h.

The National Research Council (NRC) and the Natural Resource Canada (NRCan) aim to identify and promote cost-effective approaches for net-zero industrial processes that could include renewable energy integration, energy efficiency, electrification or other technologies that replace process heating requirements.

The National Research Council (NRC), Environment and Climate Change Canada (ECCC), Health Canada (HC) and Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) are actively seeking innovative, cost-effective, safe, and scalable solutions that lead to the destruction of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) compounds across various contaminated solid or aqueous media such as water, soil, biosolids as well as spent filtration media such as granular activated carbon and resins.

Phantom Photonics aims to democratize underwater data access to improve safety, sustainability, and infrastructure management. Their focus includes preventing failures in oil and gas systems, safeguarding underwater cables, and supporting marine ecological studies. To achieve this, the Project will develop a quantum LiDAR system for Autonomous Surface Vessels (ASVs), offering high-resolution 3D imaging with SONAR-like range and LiDAR-level precision. The system will be commercialized via a Data-as-a-Service (DaaS) model for scalable, autonomous monitoring. A key goal is building a rugged, waterproof prototype integrating Phantom’s time-bin encoded quantum LiDAR, optimized for low-light and turbid conditions. Engineering efforts include pressure-resistant housing, quantum emitter integration, and embedded electronics for real-time control. Edge computing will enable onboard data processing, reducing communication needs and enhancing fleet coordination. In collaboration with NRC’s Computer Vision and Graphics (CVG) team, image analysis systems will be developed to enhance current sensor technology and to take advantage of the rapidly developing large visual and multimodal models to s
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The Project delivers the missing infrastructure for distributed quantum sensing over kilometer baselines. Ki3 Photonics will build, test, and validate a fiber-based quantum sensing backbone that spans kilometers and supports the low-jitter propagation of quantum and/or clock signals. The NRC will realize a multi-baseline, fast-timing interferometry testbed with common-clock distribution, calibrated quasi-thermal sources, and real-time correlation analysis. Jointly, the Project team will develop the stabilization infrastructure. The Project delivers a globally-unique infrastructure for distributed quantum sensing that aligns fully with the Quantum Sensors Challenge, drives Canadian innovation, and supports the development of next-generation sensing and imaging systems.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: Through ADAPT: Accessible Diagnostics for Aging-in-Place Testing will be completed in a collaboration betweenLFAnt Medical and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC) to improve access to health screening solutions for older Canadians.The project focuses on the verification of the ADAPT platform for the early detection of urinary biomarkers for chronic kidney disease (CKD), which poses disproportionate and preventable health risks among older adults. The ADAPT platform combines the NRC’s latest innovations in microfluidics-powered technologies with LFAnt medical’s next generation electromagnetic biosensors. It features a reusable reader and disposable test cartridges designed for use by minimally trained operators in community-based healthcare settings such as nursing homes and other long-term care facilities. The ADAPT platform is set to transform the paradigm of disease detection and monitoring and promote healthy aging among older Canadians.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The Project focuses on two strategic activities to enhance the operational capabilities of the CBMI AI-guided processing acceleration platform (PAP) for critical mineral extraction. The first activity involves creating a comprehensive and high-quality industrial-level dataset that documents process parameters, aiding in the development of AI models and controllers for mineral processing. The Recipient will contribute their expertise in sustainable lithium-ion battery technology and mineral processing to generate this dataset. The second activity targets optimizing mineral processing through AI models, which will automate parameter adjustments, reduce costs, and improve efficiency. These AI-driven solutions will enhance process control and contribute to the SDL's development, aligning with CBMI's objectives. The team brings expertise in materials discovery and process optimization to the Project.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The Project aims to develop the Interband-Cascade-Laser (ICL) technology, to enable the design and fabrication of ICLs in the 4.2-4.6 μm wavelength range. A portable multi-gas sensing platform will be developed using the ICLs for environmental monitoring applications. ICLs are highly desirable for tunable laser gas sensing applications due to the strong absorptions of many gas species in the mid-IR region, making them ideal for compact laser gas sensors.  In this collaborative research project, the mid-IR laser team at the NRC QN Research Centre (QNRC) will focus on developing ICL gain chips targeting 4.23 μm wavelength for CO2 sensing, while Norcada will carry out packaging and testing work to assess performance and support the ICL gain chip development work at QNRC. Norcada will further develop its tunable laser gas sensor into a portable multi-gas sensing platform and move towards commercialization by targeting air quality and continuous emission monitoring applications.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The Project aims to overcome fundamental limits in quantum communication and establish Canada as a leader in quantum networking. Today’s systems are restricted by photon loss in fibers, where the so-called PLOB bound sets the ultimate limit for secure direct communication. The project team’s approach introduces a multimode, cavity QED-based repeater architecture with chiral light-matter coupling. This enables highly efficient entanglement distribution, directional photon interactions, and multiplexed quantum memory. These advances are expected to surpass the PLOB bound by 3–5 dB over metropolitan fiber networks—representing the first experimental violation of this theoretical limit. Unlike incremental progress pursued elsewhere, the Project uniquely integrates secure quantum communication with quantumenhanced sensing and metrology within NRC’s testbed as a general quantum networking infrastructure. Potential applications include entanglement-assisted interferometry for astronomy and defense, quantum key distribution, and fault-tolerant distributed computing. By creating deployment-ready, modular infrastructure, the project directly supports Canada’s National Quantum Strategy and p
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The Project is a collaboration between Dr. Louis Gaudreau at NRC’s Quantum and Nanotechnologies Research Centre and Qubic Inc., a Canadian startup developing low-heat dissipation, broadband cryogenic amplifiers. The goal is to test and optimize a kinetic-inductance traveling-wave parametric amplifier (KITWPA) to improve the readout of superconducting microwave resonators used in NRC’s microwave-to-optical quantum transducer. Qubic will design, fabricate, and iterate to improve prototype amplifiers, while NRC will benchmark their performance on resonators coupled to spin and superconducting qubits. These KITWPAs aim to replace heat-dissipating HEMT amplifiers and support scalable quantum computing and sensing. The collaboration will validate KI-TWPAs at temperatures ranging from millikelvin to 4 Kelvin, refine amplifier integration and control electronics, and help advance the technology from TRL 6 to TRL 9, positioning Canada for leadership in quantum devices and quantum-enhanced sensing.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail:
The nanowire quantum dot photon emitter platform has reached a level of maturity where architectures for scalable entanglement distribution can be considered. To harness and deploy entangled photons in future quantum networks, components for tuning, routing, and encoding them at cryogenic temperatures are required. The project involves an on-chip solution based on actuated microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) coupled to optical systems. MEMS tailored for compatibility with the nanowire geometry will 
be designed to efficiently tune the dielectric environment of photonic structures such as waveguides. This process can enable a wide variety of devices such as path-entanglement encoders, quantum gates, and nanobeam cavities for nanowire emission enhancement. Foundry processing will be developed to fabricate devices in both the Si and SiN platforms. NRC’s capabilities in nanopositioning will be used for integrating the MEMS - nanowire components. This work will be an important resource in the evelopment of architectures for wide-scale entanglement distribution
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: While quantum magnetometers have achieved remarkable sensitivity, accuracy and stability can be key to real-world deployment. The Project exploits the quantum states of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond to target development of a portable vector magnetometer with best-in-class accuracy. Comprising experts in NV physics, quantum control and optimization, and machine learning, along with scientists from startup SBQuantum skilled in magnetometer design, calibration and deployment, SBQuantum will tackle this challenge across multiple fronts, from improved fundamental physical models to optimized control and state-of-the-art machine learning calibrations. Highly accurate vector magnetometry opens the door to stable sensor arrays that can detect and differentiate magnetic and conductive materials, a key capability for security technology. SBQuantum will also examine optimal strategies to deploy its accurate vector magnetometers for remote threat detection, laying the groundwork for commercial and defence applications that could significantly impact Canadian society.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: Effective management of organic waste reduces pollution and helps to preserve land and water resources. Organic waste (primarily food waste) in Northern landfills generate leachates polluting fragile Northern ecosystems. At the same time, such waste represents the only available source of renewable biomass in the North. The Project proposes to develop and demonstrate an innovative bioprocess for enhanced biomethane production (bioenergy) from organic food waste, which also results in the production of organic fertilizer and clean water. The proposed bioprocess is based on a combination of food waste pre-treatment in a leach-bed reactor (LBR) and enhanced biomethane production in a bioelectrochemically enhanced anaerobic digestion (BEAD) reactor based on the BEAST (bioelectrochemical anaerobic sewage treatment) concept. This concept will be tested at a pilot scale and is expected to result in a simple, robust, and low-cost system that is optimized for operation in Northern and remote communities. To reduce energy consumption and optimize BEAD performance at low temperatures, local knowledge will be used to determine locally available sources of organic waste and local microbial inoc
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: Quantum networks work best when devices exchange quantum information directly without classical conversion. The Project will study free‑space quantum networking across fixed and mobile platforms. Current quantum‑secure links using low‑flux entangled photons have limited range and rate; a new method enabling secure links at all laser flux levels aims to extend both while preserving quantum information. Custom quantum transmitters and receivers emulating squeezed light will be developed, optimized, and tested across ground and ground‑to‑airborne links using NRC’s ARTEMIS facility. System performance & stability will be studied and atmospheric effects will be evaluated through scintillometry using the ARTEMIS beam path. Although 1550 nm links cannot enetrate cloud, distributed ground stations can maintain satellite connectivity through remote clear‑sky regions. Optiwave will contribute system modeling and a novel security method, uOttawa will model atmospheric effects and provide expert staff and students and NRC provides advanced quantum‑laser expertise.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: This collaborative Project aims to develop an innovative and cost-effective beneficiation process for transforming high-carbon fly ash and legacy coal ash from landfills and ponds into high-quality cementitious materials that meet construction standards. A key focus will be the development of new second-generation passivating agents, which are essential for neutralizing residual carbon effects and will be pivotal to the Project's success. Additionally, the initiative will prioritize the creation of second-generation milling aids designed for ultra-fine milling to achieve particle sizes under 20 microns, enhancing the quality of the resulting cementitious materials. A collaboration between Prodexim, two industrial partners, the Université de Sherbrooke and the NRC, the Project seeks to establish a non-proprietary process that recycles fly ash and lowers CO2 emissions from cement production. Anticipated outcomes include a replicable process that fosters a sustainable Canadian supply of construction materials from waste, creates new job opportunities, and utilizes Canadian expertise and resources.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The project develops and demonstrates a Canadian quantum optical ground station with a Photonic Adaptive Optics System on Chip (AOSoC) for efficient free-space to fiber coupling for quantum communication internetworking. The AOSoC will be integrated into a compact optical ground station with telescope optics and fiber-based quantum output to support continuous-variable and time-bin entangled photon links. The research will characterize system performance, including phase stability, coupling efficiency, and entanglement visibility, under laboratory and field conditions. Ultimately, the project will establish a national space-to-ground, and ground-to-ground quantum communication demonstration platform for long-range quantum internetworking based on the NRC-ARTEMIS ground station to advance a commercial solution for secure, scalable quantum communication infrastructure and components for Canada.
program: Collaborative Science, Technology and Innovation Program - Collaborative R&D Initiatives

detail: The objective of this project is to identify the skills technicians need to safely and effectively install and maintain air source heat pumps in Canada. Building on prior work in Ontario, the Initial Recipient (promoter) will assess skill gaps in British Columbia and the Atlantic Regions, to provide a broader, national perspective on trades involved in heat pump installation. An updated curriculum, shaped by instructor and participant feedback, will support a Heat Pump Training Pilot Program offering subsidized green technology upskilling to at least 100 trade workers.
